scenario_text: Fatima arrives at her neighbour Maryam's house as a surprise at 2pm. Maryam opens the door in home clothes, visibly surprised. She welcomes Fatima graciously, smiles warmly, and begins bustling around to make her feel at home. Fatima smiles, assuming Maryam is thrilled by the surprise.
question_text: Maryam appears delighted and is bustling around to welcome Fatima. What is actually going on inside Maryam?
option_A: Maryam is happy that Fatima came without notice because surprise visits are treated as a stronger sign of neighbourly closeness.
option_B: Maryam is pleased by the visit but worried that Fatima may think the house looks too quiet because it is the afternoon rest period.
option_C: Maryam feels caught off guard by the surprise visit but continues acting warmly as host.
option_D: Maryam is embarrassed because she forgot a planned visit and is now trying to make it seem as if she had prepared for Fatima all along.
violated_norm: Arriving unannounced during afternoon rest time.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, guests are expected to call before visiting so the host has time to prepare the house, dress appropriately, and receive them properly. The afternoon after Dhuhr is also often treated as private rest time. Maryam's warm welcome does not mean the timing was easy for her; she is preserving hospitality while internally feeling caught off guard by the surprise arrival.
common_western_misreading: A Western visitor may read an unannounced visit as spontaneous and friendly, especially between neighbours. They may miss that in this context, arriving without notice can place pressure on the host, who must still appear gracious even if she is unprepared.

scenario_text: Ahmed visits his friend Khalid. Khalid opens the door wearing his كندورة, greets Ahmed with "يا هلا ويا مرحبا," and ushers him to the مجلس. The house is clean, بخور is burning, qahwa is ready, and dates are served. Ahmed smiles and says, 'You shouldn't have,' assuming Khalid must have made unusual preparations especially for him.
question_text: What does Ahmed get wrong about Khalid's reception?
option_A: Ahmed thinks Khalid is making the visit unusually formal because their friendship has become more distant.
option_B: Ahmed thinks Khalid is showing off the house and hospitality items to impress him.
option_C: Ahmed thinks Khalid prepared the مجلس this way because Ahmed is an unusually important guest.
option_D: Ahmed thinks Khalid made unusually special preparations for him personally.
violated_norm: Misreading standard Emirati guest reception as special personal treatment.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, receiving a guest properly includes a warm verbal welcome, a prepared مجلس, qahwa, dates, بخور, and presentable clothing such as the كندورة. These gestures do not necessarily mean the host prepared unusually for one specific person; they are part of the expected baseline of hospitality. Ahmed interprets the reception as exceptional personal effort, while Khalid is performing the normal standard of guest honouring.
common_western_misreading: A Western visitor may treat these details as unusually formal or specially arranged, because casual visits in other settings may not require the same level of preparation. They may miss that in Emirati hospitality, what looks elaborate can be the ordinary minimum for receiving a guest with respect.

scenario_text: Salem grew up mostly abroad and has only recently started hosting family gatherings again. During a majlis, he wants to show extra generosity to an elderly guest, so he serves qahwa and fills the cup more than halfway. The elder looks at the cup, takes a small sip, becomes visibly uncomfortable, and soon excuses himself from the gathering. Salem later looks worried and tells his sister, "I should have done it differently." His sister realizes Salem has misunderstood what upset the elder.
question_text: Which answer best captures the misunderstanding between Salem’s view and his sister’s understanding?
option_A: Salem thinks the elder was uncomfortable because of how the qahwa itself was prepared, while his sister understands that the issue was the amount poured into the cup.
option_B: Salem thinks the elder expected tea instead of qahwa, while his sister understands that the elder was upset because Salem served him before the younger guests.
option_C: Salem thinks the elder left because the majlis was too crowded, while his sister understands that the elder wanted Salem to sit with him instead of serving.
option_D: Salem thinks the elder was offended because the qahwa was served too late, while his sister understands that the elder was upset because the gathering ended too quickly.
violated_norm: Filling an elder’s qahwa cup too much and mistaking the reaction as a problem with the coffee.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, qahwa is normally poured in a small amount, often around one-third of the cup. A larger pour can feel excessive, careless, or socially inappropriate, especially when served to an elder in a majlis. Salem intends the larger amount as generosity, but his sister understands that the elder’s discomfort came from the breach in qahwa etiquette rather than from the taste or preparation of the coffee.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with Emirati qahwa etiquette may assume that giving more coffee shows more generosity. They may misread the elder’s discomfort as a reaction to the taste, strength, or preparation of the qahwa rather than to the amount poured.

scenario_text: Mohammed hosts guests in his majlis. He serves qahwa, dates, then a full meal, then fruit, then qahwa again. Finally, he brings out the oud/dukhoon incense burner and passes it around the room so each guest can scent their clothes. One guest, who grew up abroad, smiles and says, "You are still spoiling us," then settles back into his cushion and begins a new conversation. Mohammed looks slightly uncomfortable, realizing that his cousin has misunderstood the meaning of this final gesture.
question_text: Which answer best captures what Mohammed realizes about his cousin’s misunderstanding?
option_A: The cousin thinks the incense is meant to cover the smell of food, while Mohammed understands that the real issue is that the meal was served too late.
option_B: The cousin thinks the incense means another round of dessert or coffee is coming, while Mohammed understands that the incense is only being used to refresh the room after food.
option_C: The cousin thinks the incense is another act of continued hospitality, while Mohammed understands that the hosting sequence has been completed and the gathering is politely reaching its end.
option_D: The cousin thinks Mohammed is showing wealth through expensive incense, while Mohammed understands that the guests are expected to praise the oud before speaking again.
violated_norm: Misreading oud or dukhoon after the meal as continued hospitality instead of a polite closing cue.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, hospitality often follows a recognizable sequence: qahwa, dates, a meal, fruit, qahwa again, and then oud or dukhoon. When incense is passed after the meal, it does not mean the host is kicking guests out. Rather, it signals that the host has completed the full hospitality sequence and that the gathering is gracefully reaching its end. Mohammed understands this closing cue, while his cousin reads the incense as another reason to stay and continue the visit.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with Emirati hospitality may interpret oud or dukhoon as simply another generous gesture, missing that after the meal sequence it can function as a polite signal that guests should begin preparing to leave.

scenario_text: Saeed serves qahwa to his father's guests. He fills each cup about one-third full and stands attentively. One guest, who has just finished his cup, holds it out toward Saeed without moving it. Another guest, finished with his, gives the cup a small shake before handing it back. Saeed refills the first guest's cup and takes the second guest's cup away.
question_text: What does Saeed understand about each guest from how they handed back their cups?
option_A: Saeed understands that the first guest should be served again because he is older, while the second guest should be stopped because younger guests are expected to drink less qahwa.
option_B: Saeed understands that the first guest is silently asking for more qahwa, while the second guest is silently indicating that he has finished.
option_C: Saeed understands that both guests are finished.
option_D: Saeed understands that the first guest is unsure how to return the cup properly, while the second guest is correcting him by showing the right gesture.
violated_norm: Correctly reading the qahwa cup gestures for refill and refusal.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati hosting, the qahwa cup allows the guest to communicate silently with the server. Holding the cup out without shaking it signals that the guest would like more. Giving the cup a small shake before handing it back signals that the guest is finished. Saeed reads both gestures correctly: he refills the first guest’s cup and takes the second guest’s cup away.
common_western_misreading: A Western guest may not know that the cup movement carries a specific meaning. They might assume an empty cup should always be refilled, or that handing the cup back always means the guest is finished, missing the silent gesture system that guides qahwa service.

scenario_text: At Khalid's majlis, his young son is helping serve qahwa for the first time. The son fills a guest's cup fully. The guest takes the cup, drinks quietly, and says nothing. After the guests leave, Khalid is visibly angry with his son. The son is confused because he served the guest, the guest accepted, and no one complained. Khalid’s older brother notices the son's confusion and realizes he has misunderstood the guest's silence.
question_text: What does Khalid’s older brother realize the son believes about the guest’s reaction?
option_A: The son believes the guest drank quickly because the coffee was cold.
option_B: The son believes the guest was waiting for an apology.
option_C: The son believes the guest's silence indicated satisfaction.
option_D: The son believes the guest's silence was a test of the son's patience and hospitality.
violated_norm: Filling a guest's qahwa cup too full and mistaking the guest's silence for approval.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati hosting, qahwa is usually poured in a small amount, often around one-third of the cup. A full cup can be read as careless, disrespectful, or as a coded signal that the guest has overstayed. The guest’s silence does not mean the serving was correct; it may be a face-saving response that protects the host’s family from open embarrassment. Khalid’s son misunderstands the silence as approval, while the older brother understands that the guest likely recognized the breach and chose not to name it.
common_western_misreading: A Western reader may see the exchange as successful because the guest accepted the cup and did not complain. They may miss that in this context, silence can be a polite way of absorbing a mistake rather than evidence that no mistake occurred.

scenario_text: Mariam invites her sister-in-law and a few female cousins to lunch. When the food is brought out, one cousin politely says, "Please, don't trouble yourselves." Mariam smiles, replies, "The food is ready," and gestures to the dishes. The cousin, taking this as confirmation that there is no rush, settles back into conversation and continues talking with the others. Mariam stays standing by the table, her own seat empty. The sister-in-law shoots the cousin a quick look, but the cousin does not catch it. Eventually Mariam sits, but only after the cousin finally moves toward the table. Later that evening, Mariam tells her sister-in-law she felt the visit went poorly.
question_text: What does the sister-in-law realize the cousin misunderstood about Mariam staying by the table?
option_A: The cousin thinks Mariam is being shy about sitting first.
option_B: The cousin thinks Mariam is standing because she is waiting to bring out more food.
option_C: The cousin thinks Mariam is giving everyone more time to talk.
option_D: The cousin thinks Mariam is upset because the meal has not been praised yet.
violated_norm: Misreading the host's standing position after the meal cue as hesitation rather than formal waiting.
cultural_explanation: Customary Emirati hosting includes small acts of deference by the host. When food is ready, the host may remain standing until the guests move toward the meal and take their seats. The phrase "the food is ready" is not only information; it functions as a cue that guests should begin moving promptly. By continuing the conversation, the cousin unintentionally keeps Mariam standing in her hosting role. The sister-in-law understands this breach, but cannot correct it openly without embarrassing the cousin.
common_western_misreading: A Western guest may treat "the food is ready" as a casual update rather than an opening cue. They may think continuing the conversation is harmless, missing that the host is waiting physically and socially for the guests to respond.

scenario_text: After a long visit, Khalid stands and tells his host Salem, "Pardon me, I really must go." Salem replies warmly, "We haven't had our fill of your company." Khalid smiles, relaxes back into his seat, and begins another conversation. Salem continues smiling, but his expression tightens slightly as he realizes Khalid has misunderstood the exchange.
question_text: Which answer best captures what Salem realizes about Khalid’s misunderstanding?
option_A: Khalid thinks Salem is upset that he tried to leave, while Salem understands that guests should not mention leaving directly.
option_B: Khalid thinks Salem wants him to stay for dinner, while Salem understands that the visit should continue until the host ends it openly.
option_C: Khalid thinks Salem’s polite farewell phrase is a real invitation to keep sitting, while Salem understands that it is a courteous response to a guest preparing to leave.
option_D: Khalid thinks Salem is asking him to apologize for leaving, while Salem understands that the phrase means the guest has stayed too long.
violated_norm: Misreading a polite farewell response as a literal invitation to continue the visit.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati hospitality, a host may respond to a guest’s farewell with warm phrases such as "we have not had enough of your company" to honor the guest and avoid making them feel unwanted. This does not necessarily mean the host is asking the guest to restart the visit. Salem understands the phrase as part of a polite farewell exchange, while Khalid takes it literally and sits back down.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with Emirati farewell etiquette may interpret warm farewell phrases as literal invitations to stay longer, missing that they can function as polite, respectful responses when a visit is naturally ending.

scenario_text: At a mealtime gathering, a guest enters while everyone is eating. He says the ritual greeting "hannahum" — may they enjoy it. The hosts respond, "wa anta minhum" — and you with them. The guest smiles and finds a place to sit. A foreign visitor watching nearby whispers to Mariam, "Did he just ask them to give him food?" Mariam observes the visitor's confused expression.
question_text: What does Mariam realize the foreign visitor believes about the guest's intention?
option_A: The visitor believes the guest is warning the hosts that they should finish eating before speaking to him.
option_B: The visitor believes the guest is apologizing because he interrupted a private family meal.
option_C: The visitor believes the guest is asking to be included in the meal.
option_D: The visitor believes the guest is confused by the hosts' reply and does not know where to sit.
violated_norm: Misreading the "hannahum / wa anta minhum" exchange as a request for food.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, "hannahum" is a ritual phrase used when entering a space where others are eating. It expresses blessing or goodwill toward the people eating. The expected reply, "wa anta minhum," includes the arriving person in that blessing. The exchange is not a direct request for food, but a socially recognized script that makes the guest's entrance polite and complete.
common_western_misreading: A Western observer may not know this scripted exchange and may interpret the phrase literally or functionally, assuming the guest is asking to be fed or apologizing for interrupting.

scenario_text: Khalid enters the مجلس. He removes his نعال at the door, enters with his right foot, and gives a clear السلام عليكم to the whole room. He then walks straight to his close friend Ahmed seated on the left side of the room, shakes his hand warmly, and starts chatting with him. The eldest man in the مجلس — Ahmed's grandfather, seated at the head — watches in silence. Several of the other men exchange brief looks. Khalid notices the change in atmosphere but cannot identify what caused it.
question_text: What mistaken assumption led Khalid to greet Ahmed first?
option_A: He thought Ahmed was the person who should be greeted first because he was closest to him socially.
option_B: He thought greeting his close friend first was the warmest and most natural choice.
option_C: He thought the room was too crowded for greeting order to matter.
option_D: He thought the host would guide him if a different greeting order was expected.
violated_norm: Greeting a close friend before the eldest man in the مجلس.
cultural_explanation: The مجلس has a strict hierarchy that is enacted through the order of greeting. The eldest or highest-status person is always greeted first after the general السلام عليكم, then the rest of the room is greeted moving from right to left. This sequence is not optional — it is how respect is publicly performed in a setting where multiple generations and ranks share one room. A guest who walks past the elder to greet a peer first is not just being informal; he is publicly placing his personal connection above the elder's status. The elder cannot say anything, the other guests will not say anything, but everyone present sees the inversion. The breach lives in the room for the rest of the visit.
common_western_misreading: A Western visitor would see Khalid's entry as exemplary — he removed his shoes, gave a general greeting, and warmly engaged the person he came to see. They would interpret the silence and exchanged looks as awkwardness about something else entirely rather than as a collective registration of a hierarchy breach. They would also assume that greeting your closest connection first is universally polite, missing that in customary Emirati settings, the elder's claim on the first greeting always outranks the friend's claim.

scenario_text: Noura enters the women's مجلس at a family gathering. She gives a clear السلام عليكم to the whole room, then begins greeting from the right side of the مجلس, working her way through each woman one by one. When she reaches the left side of the room, she finally greets Umm Mohammed, the eldest woman present, who has been quietly watching from her seat near the back-left corner. Umm Mohammed returns the greeting graciously, but the other women notice the order. The host's eyes flicker briefly to her mother and back. Noura feels something is off but cannot tell what.
question_text: What mistaken assumption shapes Noura’s greeting order?
option_A: Noura thinks following the right-side order is enough, even if the eldest woman is seated elsewhere.
option_B: Noura thinks the left side should always be greeted last.
option_C: Noura thinks the general السلام عليكم already fulfilled the main greeting duty.
option_D: Noura thinks quietness during greetings means everyone is simply waiting their turn.
violated_norm: Greeting younger women before the eldest woman in the مجلس.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, greeting order in a مجلس is based first on seniority and then on direction. A guest may give a general السلام عليكم to the whole room, but the first individual greeting should go to the eldest or highest-status person, regardless of where she is seated. After that, the guest can continue through the room in the expected order. Noura followed the right-to-left pattern mechanically, but missed the higher priority rule: status comes before seat position.
common_western_misreading: A Western visitor may think the greeting was correct because Noura greeted everyone in an orderly way. They may not realize that the order of greeting carries social meaning, and that greeting younger people before the eldest can quietly signal disrespect even if everyone is eventually greeted.

scenario_text: After the meal, Khalid's son brings out the بخور and passes it to each guest one by one. The first guest leans over the smoke, scents his كندورة and غترة, and quietly begins gathering himself to stand. The second guest takes the بخور, scents his clothes, then settles back into his cushion and asks Khalid a new question about his recent trip. The son, watching from a few steps away, notices the difference between them.
question_text: What does the son understand from how each guest responds to the بخور?
option_A: The son understands that the first guest enjoyed the بخور more, while the second guest is less interested in traditional scents.
option_B: The son understands that the first guest is leaving because he disliked the conversation, while the second guest is showing greater enjoyment by staying.
option_C: The son understands that the second guest is waiting for the host to bring more بخور before deciding whether to leave.
option_D: The son understands that the first guest is preparing to leave, while the second guest is settling back into the visit.
violated_norm: Misreading بخور after a meal as continued hospitality instead of a closing cue.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati hosting, بخور offered after a meal can function as a polite closing signal. The proverb "ما بعد العود قعود" means that after the عود, guests should begin preparing to leave rather than settling into a new round of conversation. The first guest reads the cue and gathers himself to stand, while the second guest misses the signal and continues the visit.
common_western_misreading: A Western reader may interpret بخور as an extra gesture of hospitality and assume lingering afterward is appreciative, missing that it can mark the graceful end of the visit.

scenario_text: Khalid is hosting a وليمة for thirty invited men in honour of his son's graduation. As guests are arriving, his neighbour Hamad, who was not invited, walks in casually, greets the room warmly, and finds a place to sit. Khalid receives him politely and makes sure he is fed. However, throughout the meal, Hamad is served after the invited guests, seated away from the main place of honour, and not offered the better cuts of meat. Hamad assumes this is only because he arrived late.
question_text: What mistaken explanation does Hamad give for how he is being treated?
option_A: Hamad thinks Khalid is upset about a previous issue and is using the meal to show it indirectly.
option_B: Hamad thinks neighbours are always seated after relatives at a graduation وليمة.
option_C: Hamad thinks his treatment is only because he arrived late.
option_D: Hamad thinks guests who arrive late are always served last.
violated_norm: Arriving uninvited to a وليمة and being hosted without full guest honours.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, a وليمة is organized around invited guests. If someone arrives without an invitation, the host should still preserve hospitality and avoid open embarrassment, but the uninvited guest may not receive the same honours as invited guests. The proverb "لي يي بليا داعي ييلس بليا فراش" captures this position: the person is received and fed, but not fully honoured. Hamad misreads the modest treatment as a timing issue rather than a consequence of arriving uninvited.
common_western_misreading: A Western reader may interpret Hamad’s drop-in as friendly or spontaneous, and may read the host’s treatment as coincidence or personal coldness. The local norm distinguishes between being hosted politely and being honoured as an invited guest.

scenario_text: Mohammed invites his cousin Saif and a few family friends over for a simple lunch: rice, fish, and salad, with no special occasion. When the meal ends, Saif wipes his hands and says solemnly, "دفع الله الشر والبلا عمن سعى فيه." The room pauses for a brief moment. Mohammed smiles and replies, "الله يهنيك," but the men exchange small glances. Saif notices the pause and chuckles and says, 'Sorry, was I too loud?'
question_text: What mistaken explanation does Saif give himself for the room’s pause?
option_A: Saif thinks the room expected him to wait for the host to close the meal first.
option_B: Saif thinks he simply spoke too loudly after the meal.
option_C: Saif thinks the men were uncomfortable because he used a religious phrase at the table.
option_D: Saif thinks the pause showed admiration for his command of traditional phrases.
violated_norm: Using a وليمة closing phrase for an ordinary meal.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, meal-closing phrases are tied to the type of occasion. "دفع الله الشر والبلا عمن سعى فيه" is suited to a وليمة, a more formal feast involving significant preparation, while an ordinary meal calls for a lighter phrase such as "جعلها الله نعمة دايمة وحفظها من الزوال" or "أكرمكم الله." Saif misreads the pause as a volume issue, when the room is reacting to the mismatch between phrase and occasion.
common_western_misreading: A Western reader may hear both Arabic phrases as general meal thanks and miss that سنع treats them as occasion-specific scripts.

scenario_text: Khalid leaves his front door open for the breeze, but he is sitting out of sight inside the مجلس. A guest arrives and, instead of walking in, calls out "هود" and waits until Khalid replies "هدا" before entering. A foreign visitor standing nearby says, "But the door was already open. Why did he wait?" Hamad realizes the visitor has misunderstood what the guest was thinking.
question_text: What does Hamad realize the foreign visitor believes about the open door?
option_A: The visitor believes the guest was calling out because he thought Khalid had forgotten to close the door.
option_B: The visitor believes the guest was trying to test whether anyone was home before deciding whether to enter.
option_C: The visitor believes the open door already gave enough permission to enter without waiting for a reply.
option_D: The visitor believes the guest was worried Khalid was ignoring him from inside the مجلس.
violated_norm: Misreading an open door as automatic permission to enter.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, an open door does not automatically authorize entry. A guest may still call out "هود" and wait for the reply "هدا" because the host may be out of sight or not yet ready to receive someone. The exchange protects household privacy and gives the host a moment to prepare before the guest enters.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may treat an open door as a physical invitation, while the local meaning still requires social permission before entering.

scenario_text: Mark, a visiting consultant who is unfamiliar with Emirati مجلس etiquette, arrives early and sits in the centre of the مجلس. Later, an esteemed elderly Sheikh enters and is warmly seated on a side sofa so Mark is not embarrassed by being asked to move. The conversation remains inclusive and polite. However, whenever qahwa is poured or new guests enter to greet the room, they pass by Mark and serve or greet the Sheikh first before returning to him. After being passed over again during qahwa service, Mark says loudly, "Why am I being skipped if I am sitting in the middle?" The room becomes briefly awkward, and Fatima realizes Mark has misunderstood the room’s priority order.
question_text: What is the core misunderstanding in Mark's mind?
option_A: Mark assumes the server is waiting for Mark to explicitly request the coffee before pouring.
option_B: Mark assumes the server is following a strict right-to-left rule that happens to put him last.
option_C: Mark assumes the elder requested to be served first to demonstrate dominance over the younger guests.
option_D: Mark assumes his central physical location grants him priority.
violated_norm: Misreading physical centrality as مجلس priority, leading to a public interruption of serving etiquette.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, the person with age or status priority is greeted and served first, regardless of where they are seated. A host may avoid asking a guest to move in order not to embarrass them, but the room’s order still reorganizes around the elder or highest-status person. Mark mistakes the central seat for social priority, while the local norm attaches priority to the person, not the chair. The violation is serious because Mark comments loudly during qahwa service and frames respectful priority for the Sheikh as being personally skipped, creating awkwardness in a formal مجلس setting.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with مجلس etiquette may assume that physical centrality or arrival order determines who should be served first. In this context, however, qahwa service and greetings follow age, status, and مجلس priority, so serving the Sheikh first is a sign of respect rather than a personal slight against Mark.

scenario_text: Paul, an international student, arrives early to Khalid's مجلس. Khalid warmly greets Paul, shows him to a comfortable seat, and serves him qahwa. For the next 30 minutes, as more guests arrive, Khalid remains standing near the مجلس entrance instead of sitting with Paul. Paul quietly tells Mariam, "I think Khalid is avoiding me. Maybe I came too early." Mariam realizes Paul has misunderstood Khalid's behavior.
question_text: What does Mariam realize Paul believes about Khalid's internal state?
option_A: Paul believes Khalid is waiting for the oldest guest to arrive before beginning the conversation.
option_B: Paul believes Khalid is displeased with him or avoiding him because he arrived early.
option_C: Paul believes Khalid is standing because he feels physically uncomfortable sitting on the cushions.
option_D: Paul believes Khalid is standing near the entrance because he is worried about the seating arrangement.
violated_norm: Misreading a host's standing duty as personal avoidance.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, a host may remain standing near the entrance while guests are still arriving so that each person is properly welcomed, seated, and served. This is a sign of attentiveness and hospitality, not distance or displeasure toward the guest already seated. Paul applies a different hosting model, where not sitting with a guest may feel like avoidance, and therefore misunderstands Khalid's intention.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with مجلس hospitality may take the host's physical distance personally, missing that the host's posture is directed toward the whole gathering rather than toward one guest.

scenario_text: Layla is hosting a gathering of women. Instead of serving the guests herself, Layla remains seated and has her 14-year-old daughter distribute the تمر and qahwa. The daughter carefully moves from right to left, starting with the oldest guest, while Layla watches closely and nods. Chloe, an expatriate guest, watches the teenager doing the serving while the mother stays seated. Chloe frowns slightly and whispers to a friend about Layla.
question_text: What false belief does Chloe hold about Layla's internal intentions?
option_A: Chloe believes Layla is using the serving order to publicly rank the guests by importance.
option_B: Chloe believes Layla is trying to make the gathering feel more formal than necessary.
option_C: Chloe believes Layla is making her daughter serve because she wants to avoid the work herself.
option_D: Chloe believes Layla is letting her daughter serve because the older guests specifically requested it.
violated_norm: Misreading a daughter’s guest-serving role as unfair labor rather than honored mentorship.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, teaching children how to serve guests is part of transmitting hospitality, respect, and family reputation. Serving تمر and qahwa is not treated as a low-status chore; it can be an honored role that shows the child has been raised with good manners. Layla’s watching is a form of guidance and pride, not laziness or exploitation.
common_western_misreading: A Western observer may see a teenager serving while the mother sits and assume unfair labor, while the local meaning may frame it as mentorship, trust, and family pride.

scenario_text: Rashid is hosting a gathering in his مجلس, and his three teenage sons are seated in the room. A visiting guest, Salem, notices the دلة is full. Wanting to be respectful and spare his older host any effort, Salem stands, takes the دلة, and begins pouring qahwa for everyone. Rashid becomes quiet, and his sons look embarrassed.
question_text: What false belief does Salem hold about his own helpfulness?
option_A: Salem believes pouring qahwa will make him look more experienced in مجلس etiquette than the younger sons.
option_B: Salem believes the دلة is available for whoever notices it first, regardless of family role.
option_C: Salem believes Rashid wants guests to serve themselves so the family does not appear overly formal.
option_D: Salem believes taking the دلة and pouring for everyone is the helpful thing to do.
violated_norm: Taking over qahwa service from the host’s capable sons.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, when the host has capable sons present, serving qahwa to guests is part of their duty and honor-role. It shows that they can represent their father and household properly. When a guest takes the دلة and serves instead, even with good intentions, it can imply that the sons failed to perform their role.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may see pouring coffee as helpful, while the local meaning depends on who is expected to hold the دلة in the مجلس.

scenario_text: انتهت الزيارة في المجلس. أراد ديفيد، وهو ضيف أجنبي، أن يكون ضيفاً خفيفاً ومهذباً، فقرر ألا يطيل الوقوف عند الباب حتى لا يضيع وقت المضيف. وقف فجأة، وقال بسرعة وهو يتجه نحو الخارج: "شكراً جزيلاً، مع السلامة!" وغادر فوراً. شعر المضيف بجفاء واستغرب من هذا الخروج المفاجئ. في المقابل، ركب ديفيد سيارته وهو يشعر بالرضا، معتقداً أنه كان ضيفاً مثالياً يراعي وقت الآخرين.
question_text: ما الاعتقاد الخاطئ الذي يحمله ديفيد حول الحالة الذهنية للمضيف؟
option_A: يعتقد ديفيد أن المضيف سيقدّر المغادرة السريعة.
option_B: يعتقد ديفيد أن المضيف كان متعباً ويريد إنهاء الزيارة بسرعة، لذلك رأى المغادرة المختصرة تصرفاً مراعياً.
option_C: يعتقد ديفيد أن الوقوف عند الباب يعني أن المضيف ينتظر منه الخروج فوراً دون إطالة في الكلام.
option_D: يعتقد ديفيد أن الحديث عند عتبة الباب غير مناسب.
violated_norm: سوء فهم المغادرة السريعة باعتبارها مراعاة لا جفاءً في طقس الوداع.
cultural_explanation: في السنع الإماراتي، الوداع ليس مجرد خروج جسدي من المجلس، بل طقس اجتماعي ولغوي يختتم الزيارة بمودة واحترام. قد يتبادل الضيف والمضيف عبارات دعاء وشكر مثل "الله ينعم عليكم ويكثر خيركم" و"أكرمكم الله وبيض الله ويوهكم." المغادرة المفاجئة دون هذا التبادل قد تُشعر المضيف بالجفاء، حتى لو كانت نية الضيف أن يكون خفيفاً ولا يضيع وقت أحد. ديفيد يربط الأدب بالسرعة والاختصار، بينما المعنى المحلي يربط حسن الوداع بالتدرج والكلام الطيب.
common_western_misreading: الشخص الذي لا يعرف السياق الثقافي قد يظن أن الخروج السريع بعد الزيارة دليل على مراعاة وقت المضيف، ويفوته أن الوداع في السنع الإماراتي يحتاج إلى عبارات تقدير ودعاء تُشعر المضيف بأن الزيارة خُتمت باحترام.

scenario_text: زار أليكس، وهو ضيف أجنبي، منزل صديقه الإماراتي. بناءً على نصيحة تلقاها، وقف عند الباب المفتوح ونادى: "هود!" فجاءه رد بصوت عالٍ وواضح من داخل المنزل: "هدّا!" تجمد أليكس في مكانه، وشعر بالتوتر، معتقداً أن المضيف منزعج من قدومه أو أنه قاطع لحظة خاصة داخل البيت.
question_text: ما الاعتقاد الخاطئ الذي يحمله أليكس حول الحالة الذهنية للمضيف؟
option_A: يعتقد أليكس أن المضيف نسي موعد الزيارة وينادي شخصاً آخر في البيت.
option_B: يعتقد أليكس أن المضيف يستخدم صوته العالي لتحذير أهل البيت من وجود شخص غريب عند الباب.
option_C: يعتقد أليكس أن كلمة "هدّا" تعني أن عليه التراجع وعدم الدخول إلى المنزل.
option_D: يعتقد أليكس أن الصوت العالي يدل على انزعاج المضيف.
violated_norm: سوء فهم الرد العالي بـ "هدّا" باعتباره انزعاجاً لا إذناً وترحيباً.
cultural_explanation: في السنع الإماراتي، النداء بـ "هود" والرد بـ "هدّا" يحفظان خصوصية البيت وينظمان دخول الضيف. لا يكفي أن يكون الباب مفتوحاً؛ فالضيف ينادي حتى يعرف أهل البيت بوجوده، وينتظر الرد قبل الدخول. ويجب أن يكون رد "هدّا" مسموعاً بوضوح حتى يطمئن الضيف أن دخوله مأذون به. أليكس يفسر ارتفاع الصوت كغضب أو انزعاج، بينما المعنى المحلي هو الإذن والترحيب الواضح.
common_western_misreading: الشخص الذي لا يعرف السياق الثقافي قد يربط الصوت العالي بالغضب أو عدم الاستعداد، ويفوته أن الجهر بـ "هدّا" وظيفة اجتماعية عملية: إيصال الإذن للضيف ومنحه الأمان للدخول.

scenario_text: زار جون، وهو ضيف أجنبي، مجلس صديقه الإماراتي لأول مرة. أراد المضيف إكرامه بأعلى درجات الاحترام، فصب له القهوة في الفنجان حتى الثلث فقط، فيما يُعرف بصبة الحشمة، وقدمه له بابتسامة ترحيب. نظر جون إلى الكمية القليلة في قاع الفنجان وشعر بضيق خفي، معتقداً أن المضيف يتعمد تقليل ضيافته.
question_text: ما الاعتقاد الخاطئ الذي يحمله جون حول كمية القهوة التي قُدمت له؟
option_A: يعتقد جون أن المضيف يعطيه كمية قليلة لأنه يظن أنه لا يتحمل طعم القهوة القوية.
option_B: يعتقد جون أن دلة القهوة تكاد تفرغ، وأن المضيف يحاول توفير الباقي للضيوف الآخرين.
option_C: يعتقد جون أن كمية القهوة القليلة تعني بخلاً أو قلة اهتمام.
option_D: يعتقد جون أن المضيف يختبر مدى سرعته في شرب القهوة قبل أن يقرر هل يصب له مرة أخرى.
violated_norm: سوء فهم صبة الحشمة باعتبارها بخلاً لا احتراماً.
cultural_explanation: في السنع الإماراتي، صب القهوة بمقدار قليل، غالباً نحو ثلث الفنجان، ليس علامة بخل أو تقليل من شأن الضيف. بل هو صبة حشمة واحترام، تتيح للضيف أن يشرب القهوة وهي ساخنة ومناسبة، وتسمح للمضيف بتكرار الخدمة والانتباه للضيف أكثر من مرة. جون يفسر الكمية القليلة بمنطق "القليل يعني عدم كرم"، بينما المعنى المحلي هو العكس: القلة هنا جزء من أدب الضيافة.
common_western_misreading: الشخص الذي لا يعرف السياق الثقافي قد يربط الكمية القليلة بالبخل أو قلة الاهتمام، ويفوته أن صبة الحشمة تعبر عن احترام الضيف واستمرار العناية به من خلال إعادة الصب.

scenario_text: Layla, a junior Emirati employee, is sitting in a packed meeting room. When the Department Director enters and finds no empty seats, Layla immediately stands, insists the Director take her chair, and moves to stand against the wall for the rest of the meeting. Sarah, an expatriate colleague from an egalitarian culture, watches this and feels embarrassed for Layla.
question_text: What false belief does Sarah hold about Layla's internal state?
option_A: Sarah believes Layla feels pressured or diminished because she had to give up her seat to someone senior.
option_B: Sarah believes Layla is trying to avoid participating in the meeting by moving away from the table.
option_C: Sarah believes Layla is hoping the Director will personally reward her for being helpful.
option_D: Sarah believes Layla is trying to draw attention to the fact that the meeting room was poorly prepared.
violated_norm: Misreading seat-giving to a senior as pressure or humiliation.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, offering a seat to someone senior in age or rank is an expected sign of respect and good upbringing. Layla’s action is not meant as self-erasure or humiliation; it is a voluntary expression of hierarchical respect. A person unfamiliar with this norm may interpret the act through an egalitarian workplace lens and wrongly assume Layla feels embarrassed or diminished.
common_western_misreading: A person lacking this cultural understanding attributes negative emotions (fear, subservience, or ulterior motives) to Layla, completely missing the deep cultural pride driving the interaction.

scenario_text: During a team meeting, the manager proposes a timeline that Majed, a junior Emirati engineer, knows is technically impossible and could cause the project to fail. Because correcting the manager directly in front of the whole team would embarrass him, Majed stays silent, nods politely, and waits until after the meeting. He then quietly asks a trusted senior director to raise the concern with the manager in a respectful way. Liam, an expatriate colleague, overhears this and later tells others that Majed is cowardly, indirect, and political because he did not challenge the manager openly.
question_text: What false belief does Liam hold about Majed's internal motives?
option_A: Liam believes Majed is trying to build influence with the senior director before challenging the manager later.
option_B: Liam believes Majed stayed silent because he was unsure of his own calculations during the meeting.
option_C: Liam believes Majed is avoiding written responsibility by making someone else carry the message.
option_D: Liam believes Majed is acting cowardly or manipulative by going around the manager instead of speaking directly.
violated_norm: Misreading face-saving indirect communication with a senior as cowardice, manipulation, or lack of professional integrity.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati and Gulf professional سنع, directly correcting a manager in public can be face-threatening, especially when the speaker is junior. Majed is not avoiding responsibility; he is choosing a respectful channel that allows the serious technical concern to be addressed without publicly embarrassing the manager. Liam’s interpretation is serious because he judges Majed’s character and professionalism through a directness-based workplace norm, missing the local value of preserving dignity while still solving the problem.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may assume that professional honesty requires immediate public directness. This can lead them to misread respectful indirect communication as cowardice, politics, or dishonesty, when the local meaning is face-saving, hierarchy-aware problem solving.

scenario_text: During a lively family gathering, a group of young men are laughing and debating passionately. Suddenly, an elderly uncle clears his throat and begins to speak. Instantly, the young men stop talking, lower their eyes slightly, and turn their bodies in silence toward him. Tom, a visiting colleague who is unfamiliar with Emirati majlis etiquette, observes the sudden shift and feels uncomfortable, assuming the young men have become tense or afraid.
question_text: What false belief does Tom hold about the young men's internal state?
option_A: Tom believes the young men are intimidated or afraid of the elder because of the sudden silence.
option_B: Tom believes the young men are pretending to listen while silently mocking the elder.
option_C: Tom believes the young men are annoyed that the elder interrupted their conversation.
option_D: Tom believes the young men became quiet because the debate had already ended naturally.
violated_norm: Misreading respectful silence for an elder as fear or intimidation.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, young people are expected to stop side conversations and give full attention when an elder begins speaking. Lowering the gaze, turning toward the elder, and becoming quiet are signs of respect, not fear. The silence reflects proper مجلس etiquette and reverence for age. Tom’s misunderstanding is moderate because he misreads a respectful social cue as emotional discomfort or intimidation.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may interpret sudden silence around an elder as tension, fear, or loss of freedom, while the local meaning frames it as voluntary respect, attentiveness, and good upbringing.

scenario_text: John, a visiting diplomat who is unfamiliar with Emirati مجلس greeting etiquette, accompanies his Emirati colleague Mansoor to a large majlis hosted by a Sheikh. As they enter, several prominent elderly guests are sitting near the door. Mansoor gives a general greeting to the room, then walks past the men near the door and goes directly to the Sheikh to shake his hand first. John quietly stops near Mariam and whispers, "Shouldn't Mansoor greet those men first? I think he may have offended them by walking past." Mariam realizes John has misunderstood the greeting order.
question_text: What does Mariam realize John believes about the men near the door?
option_A: John believes the men near the door are Mansoor’s rivals and expected to be publicly avoided.
option_B: John believes the men near the door expected to be greeted first because Mansoor physically passed them first.
option_C: John believes the men near the door were waiting for the Sheikh to introduce Mansoor before speaking to him.
option_D: John believes the men near the door are lower-status guests whose greetings do not matter.
violated_norm: Misreading مجلس greeting order as disrespect toward guests near the door.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, entering a Sheikh’s or elder’s مجلس involves greeting the room generally, then approaching the Sheikh or highest-ranking elder first, even if other guests are physically closer to the entrance. The men near the door would not usually expect to be greeted before the Sheikh. John’s misunderstanding is mild to moderate because he applies a physical-proximity rule to a setting where greeting order follows status, seniority, and مجلس protocol.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may assume that greeting order should follow who is physically closest. In this context, however, proper etiquette gives priority to the host or highest-ranking elder, so Mansoor’s action is respectful rather than dismissive.

scenario_text: James is visiting a high-level مجلس with his Emirati colleague Saeed. When it is time to leave, Saeed does not simply say goodbye. He steps in front of the Sheikh and formally asks, "أترخّص يا طويل العمر" (May I take my leave, long-lived one). Saeed waits until the Sheikh replies "مرحبا" before raising his hands in farewell and walking out. Later, James tells Mariam, "That felt strangely authoritarian, like Saeed had to ask permission to leave." Mariam realizes James has misunderstood Saeed’s intention.
question_text: What does Mariam realize James believes about Saeed’s internal state?
option_A: James believes Saeed is asking the Sheikh for a private favor before leaving.
option_B: James believes Saeed is apologizing for something he did wrong earlier in the مجلس.
option_C: James believes Saeed feels restricted or subservient because he must ask for permission to leave.
option_D: James believes Saeed is waiting because he is unsure whether the visit has officially ended.
violated_norm: Misreading formal leave-taking from a Sheikh’s مجلس as subservience.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, leaving a Sheikh’s or elder’s مجلس involves asking permission to leave using respectful language such as "أترخّص." Waiting for the reply before departing shows respect for the status of the مجلس and the person being visited. This ritual is not meant as humiliation or coercion; it is a dignified form of proper leave-taking.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may interpret asking permission to leave as authoritarian or submissive, while the local meaning frames it as voluntary respect and good upbringing.

scenario_text: During a formal corporate meeting, David, a newly arrived expatriate HR manager who is unfamiliar with Emirati forms of respectful address, hears a young Emirati junior employee, Rashed, repeatedly address an unrelated Senior Director as "عمي" (my uncle). David frowns and later tells Mariam, "That sounded too familiar for the workplace." Mariam realizes David has misunderstood Rashed's intention.
question_text: What does Mariam realize David believes about Rashed's intention?
option_A: David believes Rashed is joking about the Senior Director's age by calling him "uncle."
option_B: David believes Rashed forgot the Senior Director's name and used a family term to hide the mistake.
option_C: David believes Rashed is being overly familiar or implying a family connection in a professional setting.
option_D: David believes Rashed is trying to pressure the Senior Director for a personal favor.
violated_norm: Misreading honorary kinship address as unprofessional familiarity.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, honorary kinship terms such as "عمي" or "خالتي" may be used for unrelated older people as respectful forms of address. In this context, Rashed is not claiming a family relationship or crossing a professional boundary; he is showing deference to an older senior figure. David’s misunderstanding is moderate because he misreads a respectful word choice as inappropriate workplace familiarity.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may interpret honorary kinship terms too literally and assume they signal unprofessional familiarity, joking, or hidden personal connection, while the local meaning is respect, warmth, and proper deference to an elder or senior figure.

scenario_text: During a high-level formal gathering, a senior leader proposes a new timeline for a project. Khalifa, an expert Emirati engineer, knows the timeline is technically impossible. Before disagreeing, Khalifa slightly lowers his gaze and formally says, "Long-lived one, if you would permit me a word." He then calmly explains the data and shows why the timeline cannot work. Robert, a visiting Western consultant, later tells Mariam, "I expected an expert to state it more directly." Mariam realizes Robert has misunderstood Khalifa’s intention.
question_text: Which answer best captures what Mariam realizes about Robert’s misunderstanding?
option_A: Robert thinks Khalifa used the respectful phrase to flatter the leader for personal advantage, while Mariam understands that Khalifa was trying to avoid taking responsibility for the data.
option_B: Robert thinks Khalifa sounded hesitant or intimidated, while Mariam understands that Khalifa was showing respect to a senior figure while still disagreeing clearly.
option_C: Robert thinks Khalifa was stalling because he had forgotten part of his data, while Mariam understands that Khalifa was waiting for someone else to speak first.
option_D: Robert thinks Khalifa avoided giving his real opinion, while Mariam understands that Khalifa agreed with the senior leader but wanted to sound polite.
violated_norm: Misreading respectful indirect disagreement with a senior figure as hesitation or lack of confidence.
cultural_explanation: In Emirati سنع and formal hierarchy norms, disagreeing with a senior person may require careful language, lowered gaze, permission-seeking, and respectful address. These cues do not necessarily mean the speaker is weak, unsure, or intimidated. Khalifa is still giving a clear expert correction, but he frames it respectfully to protect the senior leader’s dignity and preserve the formality of the setting. Robert interprets the respectful style through a more direct communication norm and mistakes it for lack of confidence.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with Emirati hierarchy norms may assume that indirect or highly respectful disagreement means the speaker is unsure, scared, or lacking authority, missing that respectful framing can be the appropriate way to challenge a senior figure.

scenario_text: Michael, a visiting exchange student who is unfamiliar with Emirati مجلس etiquette, is sitting in a crowded مجلس with a group of young Emirati men who arrived early and took comfortable seats. An elderly man arrives late. Sultan immediately stands, warmly says "تفضل يا عمي," and insists that the elder take his seat. Sultan then moves to stand near the back wall. Michael quietly tells Hamad, "I feel bad for Sultan. That was unfair." Hamad realizes Michael has misunderstood Sultan’s feelings.
question_text: What does Hamad realize Michael believes about Sultan’s internal state?
option_A: Hamad realizes Michael believes Sultan is trying to flatter the elder for personal benefit.
option_B: Hamad realizes Michael believes Sultan stood up because he was embarrassed to be sitting in the wrong place.
option_C: Hamad realizes Michael believes Sultan was already planning to leave and used the elder’s arrival as an excuse.
option_D: Hamad realizes Michael believes Sultan feels unfairly displaced or resentful after losing his seat.
violated_norm: Misreading voluntary seat-giving to an elder as unfair displacement.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, age and seniority carry social priority in the مجلس. A younger person offering a better seat to an elder, especially with respectful language such as "تفضل يا عمي," is not usually understood as humiliation or unfair loss. Sultan’s action reflects good upbringing, respect for elders, and awareness of مجلس hierarchy. Michael’s misunderstanding is moderate because he applies an arrival-order fairness model and assumes Sultan feels resentful, while the local meaning frames the act as honorable and willing.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may assume that giving up a seat means being displaced, pressured, or treated unfairly. In this context, however, the act is a voluntary sign of respect and proper مجلس etiquette.

scenario_text: رافق ديفيد، وهو ضيف غير معتاد على السنع الإماراتي، صديقه إلى مجلس الشيخ، وجلس في مقعد بارز على يسار الشيخ. لاحقاً، دخل ضيف إماراتي يُدعى سعيد، ألقى السلام العام، وصافح الشيخ أولاً. ورغم أن ديفيد كان الأقرب جسدياً لسعيد من جهة اليسار، التفت سعيد فوراً إلى اليمين وبدأ بمصافحة صف طويل من الرجال وفق ترتيب التيامن. جلس ديفيد ويده شبه ممدودة، ثم قال بصوت مسموع: "هل تجاهلتني عمداً؟ كنت الأقرب إليك." شعر بعض الجالسين بالحرج، لأن ديفيد قطع ترتيب السلام في مجلس رسمي.
question_text: ما الاعتقاد الخاطئ الذي يحمله ديفيد حول نوايا سعيد؟
option_A: يعتقد ديفيد أن سعيد تجنبه لأنه ضيف غير إماراتي.
option_B: يعتقد ديفيد أن سعيد تجاهله عمداً.
option_C: يعتقد ديفيد أن سعيد ارتبك أمام الشيخ واختار جهة اليمين عشوائياً حتى لا يبدو متردداً في المجلس.
option_D: يعتقد ديفيد أن سعيد بدأ بالجالسين على اليمين لأنهم أقاربه أو أصحاب مكانة خاصة بالنسبة إليه.
violated_norm: سوء فهم التيامن في السلام باعتباره تجاهلاً شخصياً، مع قطع ترتيب السلام في مجلس رسمي.
cultural_explanation: في السنع الإماراتي، ترتيب السلام في المجلس لا يعتمد فقط على القرب الجسدي. بعد السلام العام ومصافحة الشيخ أو الكبير، قد يبدأ الداخل بالجهة اليمنى وفق قاعدة التيامن، ثم يكمل السلام على بقية الجالسين. لذلك، الشخص الجالس على اليسار لا يُعد متجاهَلاً إذا تأخر دوره؛ مكانه محفوظ داخل ترتيب معروف. سوء الفهم هنا شديد نسبياً لأن ديفيد لا يكتفي بالشعور بالإحراج داخلياً، بل يعلّق بصوت مسموع داخل مجلس رسمي ويتهم سعيداً بالتجاهل، مما يقطع نظام السلام ويحرج الحاضرين.
common_western_misreading: الشخص غير المعتاد على هذا السياق قد يفسر تجاوز الأقرب جسدياً كإهانة أو تفضيل شخصي. في هذا الموقف، الخطأ يصبح أوضح لأن ديفيد يحوّل سوء الفهم إلى تعليق علني داخل مجلس رسمي، بينما ترتيب السلام يتبع مقام الشيخ أولاً، ثم التيامن، لا مجرد المسافة بين الأشخاص.

scenario_text: كان جون، وهو موظف أجنبي، يحضر اجتماعاً عادياً مع زملائه الإماراتيين الشباب في غرفة الاجتماعات. فجأة، دخل المدير العام للقسم، وهو رجل كبير في السن. في لحظة واحدة، هبّ الموظفون الشباب واقفين من مقاعدهم، ولم يجلسوا حتى جلس المدير. راقب جون المشهد وشعر بتوتر شديد، معتقداً أن بيئة العمل قمعية وأن زملاءه يخافون من المدير.
question_text: ما الاعتقاد الخاطئ الذي يحمله جون حول الحالة الذهنية لزملائه؟
option_A: يعتقد جون أن وقوف زملائه نابع من الخوف.
option_B: يعتقد جون أن الموظفين وقفوا لأنهم ارتكبوا خطأً جماعياً وينتظرون توبيخ المدير أمام الجميع.
option_C: يعتقد جون أن قوانين الشركة تمنع الموظفين العاديين من الجلوس قبل الإدارة العليا في أي اجتماع.
option_D: يعتقد جون أن الاجتماع انتهى فجأة بدخول المدير، وأن الجميع وقفوا استعداداً لمغادرة الغرفة.
violated_norm: سوء فهم الوقوف للكبير أو صاحب المقام باعتباره خوفاً لا احتراماً.
cultural_explanation: في السنع الإماراتي، الوقوف عند دخول كبير السن أو صاحب المقام يمكن أن يكون تعبيراً عن الاحترام والتقدير، لا علامة خوف أو خضوع. عدم الجلوس حتى يجلس الكبير يعكس ترتيباً أدبياً يحفظ مقامه أمام الحاضرين. جون يقرأ الوقوف الجماعي من خلال نموذج يربطه بالقمع الإداري، بينما المعنى المحلي يرتبط بالتوقير وحسن الأدب.
common_western_misreading: الشخص الذي لا يعرف السياق الثقافي قد يربط الوقوف الجماعي للمسؤول بالخوف أو الخضوع، ويفوته أن الوقوف للكبير أو صاحب المقام قد يكون فعلاً طوعياً يعبر عن الاحترام.

scenario_text: رافق مارك، وهو صديق أجنبي، زميله الإماراتي ماجد لزيارة والديه. عند الدخول، قبّل ماجد رأس أمه، ثم قبّل يد أبيه. بعد ذلك، اختار أن يجلس على وسادة منخفضة على الأرض بينما جلس والده على الأريكة، وبدأ يتحدث بصوت خافت ومحترم. شعر مارك بالحرج والشفقة تجاه صديقه، معتقداً أن ماجد يتصرف وكأنه مذنب أو خاضع أمام والديه.
question_text: ما الاعتقاد الخاطئ الذي يحمله مارك حول الحالة الذهنية لماجد؟
option_A: يعتقد مارك أن ماجد يحاول استعطاف والديه مادياً.
option_B: يعتقد مارك أن ماجد يشعر بالإرهاق أو المرض، لذلك جلس في مستوى منخفض وتحدث بصوت خافت.
option_C: يعتقد مارك أن والدي ماجد أجبراه على الجلوس بهذه الطريقة لأنه ارتكب خطأً قبل الزيارة.
option_D: يعتقد مارك أن ماجد يشعر بالخضوع أو الإذلال أمام والديه.
violated_norm: سوء فهم خفض الجناح للوالدين باعتباره إذلالاً لا براً واحتراماً.
cultural_explanation: في السنع الإماراتي، التعامل مع الوالدين يقوم على إظهار البر والاحترام من خلال الجسد والصوت والكلام. تقبيل رأس الأم أو يد الأب، وخفض الصوت، وعدم الجلوس في مستوى أعلى من الوالدين، كلها أفعال تعبّر عن التقدير لا عن الضعف. ماجد لا يشعر بالإذلال، بل يرى هذا السلوك جزءاً من حسن التربية والكرامة في بر الوالدين. مارك يفسر خفض الجناح من خلال نموذج يربطه بالخضوع، بينما المعنى المحلي يربطه بالمحبة والاحترام.
common_western_misreading: الشخص الذي لا يعرف السياق الثقافي قد يرى الجلوس في مستوى منخفض أو تقبيل اليد والرأس كعلامات خضوع أو خوف، ويفوته أنها في سياق بر الوالدين تعبّر عن الاحترام والمودة وحسن التربية.

scenario_text: رافق ليام، وهو صديق أجنبي، زميله سلطان لزيارة والده. كانت هناك أريكة مرتفعة ومريحة وكرسي منخفض. بادر سلطان بالجلوس على الأريكة المرتفعة وترك الكرسي المنخفض لوالده. لاحظ ليام أن الوالد أصبح صامتاً وبدا عليه عدم الارتياح، لكنه اعتقد أن الوالد شخص يصعب إرضاؤه أو أنه يشعر بالتعب من الزيارة.
question_text: ما الاعتقاد الخاطئ الذي يحمله ليام حول سبب ضيق الوالد؟
option_A: يعتقد ليام أن ضيق الوالد نابع من مزاج شخصي.
option_B: يعتقد ليام أن الوالد منزعج لأنه يفضّل الجلوس وحده ولا يريد أن يكون سلطان قريباً منه أثناء الزيارة.
option_C: يعتقد ليام أن الوالد صامت لأنه متعب جسدياً أو غير مرتاح من طول الزيارة.
option_D: يعتقد ليام أن الوالد يريد من سلطان أن يجلس بجانبه تحديداً، لا في مواجهته أو في مكان مختلف من الغرفة.
violated_norm: سوء فهم ضيق الوالد باعتباره مزاجاً شخصياً لا ردّ فعل على جلوس الابن أعلى منه.
cultural_explanation: في السنع الإماراتي، المستوى الجسدي في الجلوس قد يحمل معنى احترامياً، خاصة مع الوالدين أو كبار السن. جلوس الابن في مكان أعلى أو أكثر راحة وترك والده في مقعد أدنى قد يُفهم كإخلال بترتيب الاحترام، حتى لو لم يقصد الابن الإساءة. الوالد لا يحتاج إلى التصريح بضِيقه؛ فالصمت وعدم الارتياح قد يكونان كافيين لتسجيل أن السنع لم يُراعَ. ليام يفسر الضيق كصفة شخصية أو تعب، بينما المعنى المحلي مرتبط بمكانة الوالد وطريقة الجلوس أمامه.
common_western_misreading: الشخص الذي لا يعرف السياق الثقافي قد يرى اختيار المقاعد مسألة راحة شخصية فقط، ويفوته أن الارتفاع والمكان في الجلوس يمكن أن يعبّرا عن احترام الكبير أو الإخلال بمقامه.

scenario_text: كان الوالد يشرح قصة تاريخية، فقاطعه ابنه راشد ليصحح له تاريخاً خاطئاً بأسلوب مباشر أمام الضيوف. صمت الوالد فجأة وظهر عليه الانزعاج. كان جاك، وهو صديق أجنبي، حاضراً، وشعر بالرضا تجاه راشد، معتقداً أنه كان ذكياً ودقيقاً وساعد والده على تصحيح المعلومة أمام الجميع.
question_text: ما الاعتقاد الخاطئ الذي يحمله جاك حول الحالة الذهنية للوالد في هذه اللحظة؟
option_A: يعتقد جاك أن الوالد سيقدّر دقة راشد أمام الضيوف.
option_B: يعتقد جاك أن الوالد صمت لأنه يحاول تذكر بقية القصة بعد أن ساعده راشد في تصحيح التاريخ.
option_C: يعتقد جاك أن الوالد يشعر بالفخر لأن ابنه بدا مثقفاً ودقيقاً أمام الضيوف.
option_D: يعتقد جاك أن الوالد سمح لراشد بتولي الحديث لأنه يعرف تفاصيل القصة بشكل أفضل.
violated_norm: سوء فهم تصحيح الابن العلني لوالده باعتباره مساعدة لا إحراجاً.
cultural_explanation: في السنع الإماراتي، مقاطعة الكبير، وخاصة الوالد، وتصحيحه علناً أمام الضيوف قد تُعد قلة احترام لأنها تنتقص من هيبته في المجلس. حتى لو كانت المعلومة التي قالها الابن صحيحة، فالطريقة مهمة: يُتوقع من الابن أن ينتظر حتى ينتهي والده، أو يستأذن بلطف قبل أن يضيف أو يصحح. جاك يركّز على دقة المعلومة، بينما المعنى المحلي يركّز على حفظ مقام الوالد أمام الآخرين.
common_western_misreading: الشخص الذي لا يعرف السياق الثقافي قد يطبّق نموذجاً يرى أن قول الحقيقة مباشرة أهم من ترتيب الكلام وحفظ الهيبة، ويفوته أن التصحيح العلني للكبير قد يُفهم كإحراج حتى عندما تكون النية حسنة.

scenario_text: During a long conversation in the مجلس, the host needs to reach for something behind him. Instead of turning his back, he shifts sideways and keeps his body angled toward the guest. An expatriate guest, Chris, notices the awkward movement and thinks the host suddenly seems uneasy.
question_text: What false belief does Chris hold about the host's internal state?
option_A: Chris believes the host is uncomfortable because he wants the conversation to end.
option_B: Chris believes the host is acting nervous or suspicious and may be hiding something.
option_C: Chris believes the host is trying to signal privately to someone outside the room.
option_D: Chris believes the host is physically uncomfortable and trying to stretch without interrupting the conversation.
violated_norm: Misreading the host's sideways movement as nervousness instead of guest respect.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, a host avoids turning his back to a seated guest. If he needs to reach for something, he may shift sideways, angle his body, or ask for help instead of fully turning away. This is a physical performance of respect, not nervous or suspicious behavior.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may interpret the awkward movement as uneasiness or secrecy, missing that the host is maintaining respect by avoiding showing his back to the guest.

scenario_text: Ahmed visits his friend in the hospital. He stays for about ten minutes, says "مأجور وأجر وعافية," and leaves. A nurse from a different culture watches this and later says, "That was such a short visit. Maybe he does not really care."
question_text: What does the nurse misunderstand about Ahmed's intention?
option_A: The nurse thinks Ahmed left quickly because he felt uncomfortable around illness.
option_B: The nurse thinks Ahmed’s short visit means he does not care deeply about the patient.
option_C: The nurse thinks Ahmed only came because someone pressured him to visit.
option_D: The nurse thinks Ahmed was following a strict hospital rule about visitor timing.
violated_norm: Misreading a brief hospital visit as lack of care.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, visiting the sick is important, but the visit is often kept brief so the patient can rest. Saying "مأجور وأجر وعافية" expresses care and prayer for recovery. In this context, leaving after a short visit can be considerate rather than cold.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may assume that a longer visit shows more care, while the local norm may treat a brief, prayerful visit as more respectful of the patient’s comfort.

scenario_text: Hamad is hosting a وليمة for invited guests. His neighbor Saleh drops in without having been invited. Hamad smiles warmly, offers him qahwa, seats him at the side, and makes sure he is included in the meal. Saleh leaves feeling fully welcomed. Later, Hamad’s wife asks whether Saleh had been invited. Hamad quietly shakes his head no.
question_text: What is Hamad most likely thinking beneath his warm treatment of Saleh?
option_A: Hamad is worried that he forgot to invite Saleh and is relieved Saleh came anyway.
option_B: Hamad is pleased because Saleh’s arrival shows that the neighborhood has a relaxed and open social atmosphere.
option_C: Hamad privately recognizes that Saleh arrived awkwardly, even though he treats him warmly.
option_D: Hamad believes Saleh came to test whether the family had prepared enough food for unexpected guests.
violated_norm: Attending a وليمة without invitation while the host must still preserve hospitality.
cultural_explanation: In Emirati سنع, a host is expected to receive a guest generously, even if the guest arrives unexpectedly or awkwardly. This can create a layered situation: the host may privately recognize that a boundary was crossed while outwardly maintaining warmth, qahwa, food, and inclusion. The proverb "لي يي بليا داعي ييلس بليا فراش" captures the awkward position of the uninvited guest, but a skilled host conceals discomfort during the act of hosting.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may read Hamad’s warmth as simple pleasure, missing that hospitality can coexist with private awareness of a social breach.

scenario_text: Reem and Saeed are co-presenting a project to potential business partners. When a senior male guest enters, Reem steps back slightly while Saeed steps forward to handle the handshake and introductions. Thomas, a foreign business partner, notices the shift and later tells a colleague, "It looked like Saeed took over the room."
question_text: What misinterpretation is Thomas making about the mental dynamic between Reem and Saeed?
option_A: Thomas believes Saeed is trying to impress the senior guest by making himself appear more important than Reem.
option_B: Thomas believes Reem is being excluded or pushed aside by Saeed.
option_C: Thomas believes Reem forgot the correct introduction protocol and Saeed had to correct her mistake.
option_D: Thomas believes Saeed and Reem are avoiding the senior guest because they are uncomfortable with the meeting.
violated_norm: Misreading coordinated mixed-gender professional etiquette as exclusion.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, a male colleague may handle the handshake and initial greeting with a senior male guest when the female colleague has not initiated contact. This can be a mutually understood way of respecting modesty norms in a professional setting, not an act of silencing or exclusion.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may read Reem’s step back as suppression, while the local interpretation may see it as coordinated professional etiquette.

scenario_text: At a traditional Emirati wedding, men and women celebrate in separate areas. A male caterer accidentally enters the women’s section while looking for the service area. The women immediately adjust their عبايات, some turn slightly away, and others move to the side to restore the privacy of the space. Sarah, a visiting guest who is unfamiliar with gender-separated wedding customs, watches the scene and thinks the women have suddenly become frightened or personally threatened.
question_text: What false belief does Sarah hold about the women's internal state?
option_A: Sarah believes the women are panicking or feeling personally threatened by the caterer’s presence.
option_B: Sarah believes the women are embarrassed because they were not ready to be seen by wedding staff.
option_C: Sarah believes the women are angry because the caterer entered without greeting the host first.
option_D: Sarah believes the women are leaving because the wedding program is about to move to another room.
violated_norm: Misreading modesty adjustments after an accidental boundary crossing in a gender-separated space as panic or fear.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع around gender-separated celebrations, the women's section is treated as a private space. The women have not violated a norm; rather, they are calmly restoring the boundary after an unrelated man enters by mistake. Adjusting عبايات, turning slightly away, or moving aside can be modesty responses, not signs of panic or danger. Sarah’s misunderstanding is moderate because she misreads a culturally expected privacy response as fear or personal threat.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with gender-separated event norms may interpret sudden modesty adjustments as panic, fear, or danger. In this context, however, the local meaning is a calm response to an accidental privacy boundary crossing.

scenario_text: Nadia introduces her colleague Dana to a male visitor from abroad. The visitor extends his hand. Dana smiles politely, greets him verbally, but does not extend her hand. The visitor pauses, withdraws his hand awkwardly, and becomes quieter for the rest of the meeting. Nadia notices his reaction and realizes he has misunderstood Dana’s intention.
question_text: Which answer best captures what Nadia realizes about the visitor’s misunderstanding?
option_A: The visitor thinks Dana’s refusal to shake hands was a personal rejection, while Nadia understands that Dana was maintaining a modesty boundary.
option_B: The visitor thinks Dana did not notice his hand, while Nadia understands that Dana was distracted by the meeting.
option_C: The visitor thinks Dana avoided the handshake because he has lower professional status, while Nadia understands that she was waiting for a formal introduction.
option_D: The visitor thinks Dana follows the same no-handshake rule with everyone, while Nadia understands that Dana was upset with him personally.
violated_norm: Misreading a modesty-based handshake boundary as personal rejection.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati and Islamic modesty norms, some women may choose not to shake hands with unrelated men while still greeting them warmly and professionally through speech, eye contact, or polite acknowledgment. Dana’s action is not meant as personal rejection, but the visitor interprets it through a different greeting norm. Nadia understands that the visitor has mistaken a modesty boundary for coldness or dislike.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with Emirati or Islamic modesty norms may assume that refusing a handshake means rejection, rudeness, or lack of professionalism, missing that a respectful verbal greeting can replace physical contact.

scenario_text: Two Emirati men greet each other at a gathering by briefly touching noses, a gesture known as موايهة بالخشوم. A visiting tourist who is unfamiliar with Emirati male greeting customs sees the gesture and feels unsure how to interpret the physical closeness. Later, he says quietly, "I didn’t realize that was a normal respectful greeting here."
question_text: What false belief does the tourist hold about the men's relationship?
option_A: The tourist believes the men are using the gesture to confirm they belong to the same tribe.
option_B: The tourist believes the men are testing each other's personal space before speaking.
option_C: The tourist believes the men are showing that one man has higher social status than the other.
option_D: The tourist believes the men are being unusually intimate.
violated_norm: Misreading موايهة بالخشوم as unusual intimacy instead of a customary respectful male greeting.
cultural_explanation: In customary Emirati سنع, موايهة بالخشوم is a brief nose-to-nose greeting used by men as a sign of respect, brotherhood, and social closeness within an accepted cultural register. It is not romantic, inappropriate, or unusually intimate. The misunderstanding is mild to moderate because the tourist privately misreads a normal greeting cue, but does not openly insult or disrupt the interaction.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this greeting may focus on the physical closeness and misread it as unusual intimacy. In this context, however, the gesture functions as a respectful Emirati male greeting that communicates warmth, familiarity, and social respect.

scenario_text: At a family gathering, a male cousin reaches the doorway of a room where women are sitting. Before entering, he calls out "هود هود" and waits until someone inside replies "هدا هدا." The women calmly adjust their عبايات and shawls before he passes through toward the men's section. His foreign-raised brother watches this and later says, "That felt like he was making them nervous." Mariam realizes he has misunderstood the cousin's intention.
question_text: What does Mariam realize the foreign-raised brother believes about the cousin's intention?
option_A: Mariam realizes he believes the cousin is announcing himself because he thinks the women are disorganized and need to be warned before he enters.
option_B: Mariam realizes he believes the cousin is using an old-fashioned phrase to make himself look more traditional in front of the family.
option_C: Mariam realizes he believes the cousin is asking permission from the older men nearby.
option_D: Mariam realizes he believes the cousin's call is about the women's fear or discomfort.
violated_norm: Misreading هود هود as creating fear instead of respecting gendered space.
cultural_explanation: In Emirati سنع, a man may call out before entering a room or passing through a space where women may be sitting. The reply gives women time to adjust or signal that he may enter. This is a routine modesty practice, not a sign that the women fear or dislike him.
common_western_misreading: A Western observer may misread the call-and-response as dramatic, controlling, or fear-based, instead of understanding it as a respectful warning before entering a gendered family space.

scenario_text: At a corporate event photo opportunity, the Emirati women in the team gracefully step out of the frame when a foreign male colleague tries to join the photo. He feels visibly hurt, thinking they personally dislike him or do not want to be associated with him publicly.
question_text: What mistaken belief is driving the colleague's hurt reaction?
option_A: He believes the women stepped away because they personally dislike being associated with him.
option_B: He believes Emirati women are never allowed to appear in professional photographs.
option_C: He believes the women were unhappy with the photographer’s instructions.
option_D: He believes the women were avoiding the company’s public image campaign.
violated_norm: Misreading a modesty-based photo boundary as a personal rejection.
cultural_explanation: Some Emirati women observe a personal or customary modesty norm of not appearing in photos with unrelated men, especially in professional or public contexts. Stepping out of the frame can be a quiet way of maintaining this boundary without embarrassing anyone.
common_western_misreading: A person unfamiliar with this norm may personalize the action as rejection, or overgeneralize it into the false belief that Emirati women cannot be photographed at all.

scenario_text: تدخل ضيفة جديدة إلى مجلس النساء. بسبب ارتباكها، لا تلقي تحية عامة واضحة على المجلس كله في البداية، بل تتجه مباشرة إلى أكبر امرأة في المجلس، تسلم عليها بحرارة، ثم تنتقل من اليمين إلى اليسار للسلام على البقية. بعض النساء يلاحظن أن التحية العامة كان يمكن أن تكون أوضح، لكن ترتيب السلام على الكبيرة أولاً يُفهم بوصفه احتراماً. ضيفة أجنبية في الغرفة تشعر بالاستياء، معتقدة أن الضيفة الجديدة كانت فظة لأنها تجاوزت النساء الجالسات قرب الباب وذهبت مباشرة إلى السيدة الكبيرة في الزاوية البعيدة.
question_text: ما الاعتقاد الخاطئ الذي تحمله الضيفة الأجنبية حول نوايا الضيفة الجديدة؟
option_A: تعتقد الضيفة الأجنبية أن الضيفة الجديدة تعرف السيدة الكبيرة فقط، لذلك بدأت بها لأنها جاءت لزيارتها تحديداً.
option_B: تعتقد الضيفة الأجنبية أن الضيفة الجديدة كانت مستعجلة، فتجاوزت النساء القريبات من الباب دون قصد.
option_C: تعتقد الضيفة الأجنبية أن تجاوز النساء القريبات من الباب كان فظاظة.
option_D: تعتقد الضيفة الأجنبية أن الضيفة الجديدة أخطأت في تطبيق السنع.
violated_norm: سوء فهم البدء بالأكبر سناً باعتباره تجاهلاً للنساء الأقرب إلى الباب، مع وجود ضعف بسيط في التحية العامة للمجلس.
cultural_explanation: في السنع الإماراتي، عند الدخول إلى المجلس، تكون أولوية السلام للأكبر سناً أو الأعلى مقاماً، بغض النظر عن مكان جلوسها. لذلك، اتجاه الضيفة الجديدة إلى السيدة الكبيرة أولاً ليس تجاهلاً للنساء القريبات من الباب، بل يعكس احترام السن والمقام. مع ذلك، كان من الأفضل أن تبدأ بتحية عامة واضحة للمجلس كله قبل التوجه للسلام الفردي، لذلك توجد مخالفة بسيطة في طريقة الدخول، لا في ترتيب السلام نفسه. الضيفة الأجنبية تفسر تجاوز النساء القريبات كفظاظة، بينما المعنى المحلي الأساسي هو احترام ترتيب السن والمكانة.
common_western_misreading: الشخص الذي لا يعرف السياق الثقافي قد يفترض أن السلام يجب أن يبدأ بمن هو أقرب جسدياً إلى الباب. في هذا السياق، الأولوية في المجلس تُبنى على السن والمقام قبل القرب المكاني، مع أهمية أن ترافق ذلك تحية عامة واضحة حتى لا يبدو الدخول مفاجئاً أو ناقصاً.

scenario_text: التقى رجلان إماراتيان في المجلس فتبادلا الموايهة بالخشوم ثم تبادلا عبارات الترحيب. كان زائر أجنبي حاضراً، فشعر بالاستغراب واعتقد أن التحية حميمة جداً أو غير مناسبة في مكان رسمي. لاحظ حمد ارتباكه، وأدرك أنه أساء فهم معنى التحية.
question_text: ما الذي يدركه حمد عن اعتقاد الزائر الأجنبي تجاه الموايهة بالخشوم؟
option_A: يدرك حمد أن الزائر يعتقد أن الرجلين لا يعرفان طريقة المصافحة.
option_B: يدرك حمد أن الزائر يعتقد أن التحية حميمة أو غير مناسبة رسمياً.
option_C: يدرك حمد أن الزائر يعتقد أن الرجلين من العائلة نفسها فقط.
option_D: يدرك حمد أن الزائر يعتقد أن الرجلين يتجنبان المصافحة بسبب خلاف بينهما.
violated_norm: سوء فهم الموايهة بالخشوم باعتبارها حميمية زائدة لا تحية رجالية مألوفة.
cultural_explanation: في السنع الإماراتي، الموايهة بالخشوم تحية رجالية معروفة، تقوم على تلامس خفيف وسريع بين أطراف الأنوف، وتعبّر عن الاحترام والأخوة الاجتماعية. استخدامها في المجلس لا يعني حميمية غير مناسبة ولا جهلاً بالمصافحة، بل هو شكل أصيل من أشكال السلام والترحيب بين الرجال. حمد يفهم أن الزائر قرأ القرب الجسدي بطريقة خاطئة، بينما المعنى المحلي هو الاحترام والتآلف.
common_western_misreading: الشخص الذي لا يعرف السياق الثقافي قد يرى قرب الوجه في هذه التحية غريباً أو حميماً أكثر من اللازم، ويفوته أنها في السنع الإماراتي تحية رجالية مألوفة تحمل معنى الاحترام والأخوة.

scenario_text: في اجتماع عمل، يقف رجل إماراتي أمام زميلة إماراتية. يبتسم لها باحترام، يحييها بالكلام، ولا يمد يده للمصافحة حتى لا يفرض عليها تفاعلاً جسدياً قد لا يناسب حدود السنع. زائر غير معتاد على السنع الإماراتي يلاحظ ذلك، فينزعج ويهمس لمريم: "يبدو أنه متعالٍ أو لا يريد التعامل معها مهنياً." لاحظت مريم انزعاج الزائر، وأدركت أنه أساء فهم نية الرجل.
question_text: ما الذي تدركه مريم عن اعتقاد الزائر تجاه نية الرجل؟
option_A: تدرك مريم أن الزائر يعتقد أن الرجل لا يعرف الزميلة جيداً.
option_B: تدرك مريم أن الزائر يعتقد أن الرجل غاضب من الزميلة بسبب خلاف سابق.
option_C: تدرك مريم أن الزائر يعتقد أن الرجل ينتظر إشارة من المدير قبل أن يبدأ التحية.
option_D: تدرك مريم أن الزائر يعتقد أن الرجل متعالٍ أو رافض للزميلة.
violated_norm: سوء فهم عدم مبادرة الرجل بالمصافحة باعتباره تعالياً أو رفضاً بدلاً من احترام حدود التفاعل بين الجنسين.
cultural_explanation: في السنع الإماراتي، قد يتجنب الرجل المبادرة بالمصافحة الجسدية مع المرأة غير المحرم، ويكتفي بتحية لفظية محترمة أو إيماءة مهذبة حتى لا يفرض عليها تفاعلاً جسدياً. هذا لا يعني أنه يتجاهل المرأة أو يقلل من مكانتها المهنية، بل يعبر عن احترامه لحدود التعامل بين الجنسين. سوء الفهم هنا متوسط الخطورة لأن الزائر يفسر سلوكاً احترامياً على أنه تعالٍ أو رفض مهني، مما قد يخلق حكماً غير عادل على نية الرجل.
common_western_misreading: الشخص غير المعتاد على هذا السياق قد يربط الاحترام المهني بالمصافحة المباشرة فقط، ويفوته أن التحية غير اللمسية يمكن أن تحمل المعنى نفسه من الاحترام. في هذا السياق، عدم المبادرة بالمصافحة ليس إقصاءً للمرأة، بل مراعاة لحدود التفاعل وترك مساحة للطرف الآخر.
